original_abstract:
Document-level sentiment classification typically struggles to capture the complex semantic relationships between sentences within a larger text. To address this, we propose a bottom-up neural network framework designed to learn robust vector-based document representations. Our model follows a hierarchical approach: it first generates sentence-level representations using either Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) or Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks; subsequently, it employs a gated recurrent neural network to adaptively encode the meanings of these sentences and their intrinsic relations into a final document vector. 

We evaluated our model on four large-scale review datasets from IMDB and the Yelp Dataset Challenge. Our experimental results demonstrate two key findings: first, our tiered neural architecture significantly outperforms several state-of-the-art algorithms; second, gated recurrent units prove far more effective than standard recurrent neural networks for modeling the composition of documents in sentiment analysis tasks.
humanized_abstract:
Document-level sentiment classification is a challenging task which has proved to be hard to catch the underlying semantic relationships between different sentences expressed in a document. To resolve the problem mentioned above, we propose a bottom-up neural network framework which can effectively learn vector representations for documents. For our approach, we use a hierarchical structure. In the first level, we apply techniques including CNN or LSTM to get the vector representation for each sentence. Then in the second level, the adaptive gated recurrent network(GRNN) is used to model the semantics of the sentences and the intrinsic relationships between the sentences so as to get the vector representation for the whole document.

We experimented with our model on four large-scale review datasets from IMDB and the Yelp Dataset Challenge. Experimental results show that 1) our tiered deep learning model outperforms most of the state-of-the-art approaches; 2) GRU is much better than RNN in this task due to its strong capability in modeling the compositionality of documents.
